protocol: medical - respiratory distress/asthma/copd/croup/reactive airway (respiratory distress - croup/epiglottits) (protocol 9 - 11)

dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for Breathing Problems at --Address Redacted--.
Hello, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911 today?
I've been having trouble breathing since this morning. It's not getting much better.
Morgan, can you tell me your name and where we are?
Uh, yes. I'm Morgan. We're in [CITY].
What month is it today, Morgan?
It's April. I think.
Morgan, can you tell me how you're feeling otherwise? Any pain or discomfort besides breathing issues?
Morgan is alert and oriented, giving accurate answers. Her GCS is 15.

She's breathing at 18 per minute, not tachypneic. No signs of hypoxic symptoms. Significant expiratory wheezes bilaterally in upper and lower lobes. No tripoding or accessory muscle use. She can speak full sentences without difficulty.
Pulse is 140, strong, and regular.
Morgan, can you tell me more about the shortness of breath and tightness?
I feel like my chest is really tight and it's hard to take a deep breath. The albuterol I used today gave some relief but it's not lasting long.
What medications are you currently taking?
Albuterol, Vyvanse, and Abilify.
Do you have any pre-existing conditions?
I was diagnosed with mild asthma when I was younger.
When was the last time you ate or drank something?
I had a granola bar and some water about an hour ago.

Morgan is not showing signs of distress, diaphoresis, or pallor. She is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation (AOx4). Her work of breathing is mild, and she is able to speak in full sentences.
Pulse is at 140 beats per minute and regular.
Respirations are at 18 per minute. You're able to speak in full sentences, which is reassuring.
Blood pressure is 107/77.
SpO2 is at 95%.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia.
Temperature is 100.0 Fahrenheit tympanic.
Blood glucose could not be obtained at this time.
We're going to do a nebulizer treatment with Albuterol and Atrovent. I'm also putting you on oxygen to help with the breathing.
Morgan, we are going to give you 5 mg of Albuterol and 0.5 mg of Ipratropium via nebulizer.
I'm putting you on 6 liters per minute of oxygen now. You may feel a little cool on your nose.
We'll also be doing a quick 4-lead EKG to monitor your heart rhythm.
Pulse is now 141, respirations remain 18 per minute. Vitals are otherwise unremarkable.
BP is now 89/68, which is a slight decrease.
SpO2 is up to 99%. That's a positive sign.
You're reacting well to the treatment and reporting it's helping some, right?
Yeah, the breathing is easier now, but I still feel that tightness in my chest.
We're following the asthma protocol, so we'll continue monitoring and administer further care as needed.
Morgan, we're going to take you to [COMPANY_NAME] for further evaluation and treatment. Is that okay with you?
Morgan, we're going to help you get into the stretcher to get you ready for transport.
Okay. Just be careful.
Of course. We'll make sure everything's secure for the ride.
All straps and rails have been fastened, and we're ready to move to the truck.
Morgan, you're doing well. You mentioned not being in pain-is that right?
Yeah, I've had chest tightness, but no pain at all.
You haven't seemed to have any signs of being pale, and can still talk in full sentences, which is reassuring.
Morgan, were you ever having trouble speaking in full sentences during your shortness of breath earlier?
No. I was able to speak full sentences, just felt like I was out of breath.
That reassures us that your breathing hasn't become severe.
We've got you now. We don't stop until we hit the ER.
We're making good progress.
We are en route to [COMPANY_NAME] now. We provided Albuterol and Ipratropium via nebulizer and assisted oxygen at 6LPM. Vital signs are stable, and she continues to report improvement.
On arrival, we'll transfer care to the pediatric emergency department at [COMPANY_NAME].
